This is a fantastic modern gallery, packed with a wide range of interesting and thought-provoking works of art. It is very spacious and well designed, with a natural flow between the rooms. The bathrooms were spotless. This museum sits in a wonderful parkland and is a joy to explore. The restaurant is also excellent, serving tasty food with really good portions. There is a viewing tower accessible by lift that provides amazing views of the city. Finally, big shout out to all the staff who clearly work hard to make this a wonderful place for tourists like me to visit. Thank you!

Founded in 1895, de Young Museum in San Francisco is one of the oldest and largest art museums in the United States. Located at 50 Hagiwara Tea Garden Drive, along with the Legion of Honor in Lincoln Park, which was founded in 1924, de Young makes up the Fine Arts Museum of San Francisco. Open from 9:30 to 5:15 Tuesday through Sunday, it showcases an extraordinary permanent collection of American art from the 17th century through the 21st century, featuring modern and contemporary art, photography, international textile and costumes and art from Africa, Oceania and the Americas. The 9th floor observation level of de Young's Hamon Tower offers breathtaking 360-degree views of San Francisco and the Pacific Ocean. Current exhibits include Art of Manga, Arts of Indigenous America, Rose B. Simpson, Art by Bay Area Artists, Leilah Babirye, Contemporary Painting in Papua, North Guinea, Andy Goldsworthy's Drawn Stone and James Turrell's Three Gems.

Came for the Manga Special Exhibit! We knew very little about Manga before our visit at the DeYoung. We enjoyed it so much that we made a second visit a few days later so we could spend more time in the exhibit. I personally loved the panels dedicated to The Solitary Gourmet. My husband liked One Piece so much he bought a book in the gift shop and has been enjoying the challenge of "reading" the graphic novels. The rest of the museum has a well curated collection. I only wish there were more benches in the various galleries where visitors can sit and enjoy the art.  Lunch in the cafe was good, but not cheap. We did not visit this museum as part of a tour and I'm not sure why TA is so insistent on identifying a tour here. This is an easy museum to do on your own!
